opportunity_overview.title: VRS AI Chatbot Solution to Enhance Agency Recruitment Efforts
opportunity_overview.office: Interior Business Center, Acquisition Services Directorate (IBC/AQD), Office of Volunteer Recruitment and Service (VRS)
source_documents: RFQ 140D0426Q0324_Q&A.txt - Amendment 1 Contractor Questions and Government Responses (33 pages)
RFQ Contract Amendment.txt - Standard Form 30 Amendment/Modification (Amendment 1, effective 04/03/2026)
peacecorps_aichatbot.txt - Performance Work Statement (PWS), dated March 16, 2026 (27 pages)

contract_details.contract_type: Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP), Purchase Order; single vendor award
contract_details.set_aside: Total Small Business (only small businesses will be considered; FAR 52.219-14 Limitations on Subcontracting applies; small business prime must perform >50% of labor)
contract_details.estimated_value: Not publicly disclosed; Government confirmed a budget has been allocated for both the base year and the full period of performance but will not disclose specifics
contract_details.period_of_performance: Base Period: June 01, 2026 to May 31, 2027; Option Period 1: June 01, 2027 to May 31, 2028; Option Period 2: June 01, 2028 to May 31, 2029; Option Period 3: June 01, 2029 to May 31, 2030; Option Period 4: June 01, 2030 to May 31, 2031; Total not to exceed five (5) years if all options exercised
contract_details.place_of_performance: Contractor site located within the United States of America; all work is expected to be performed off-site (remote); no offshore or nearshore resources permitted

requirements_summary.scope_overview: The Peace Corps seeks a FedRAMP High authorized, commercially available SaaS conversational AI chatbot solution to be deployed on peacecorps.gov, enhancing public-facing recruitment, outreach, and engagement by answering inquiries, routing users, and supporting live agent escalation for the Office of Volunteer Recruitment and Service (VRS).
key_requirements: FedRAMP High Authorization required for the entire solution (chatbot application and platform infrastructure); solution must be FedRAMP High authorized or actively in-process for FedRAMP High designation at time of quote submission; FedRAMP Moderate will NOT be accepted

Commercially available SaaS conversational AI solution; hybrid architectures acceptable but all components must meet FedRAMP High designation; offeror may serve as system integrator deploying a FedRAMP-authorized third-party platform

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture required; AI must rely solely on Peace Corps-curated dataset (estimated 1,000-10,000 pages at initial deployment); Peace Corps will provide all RAG source data as GFI; contractor provides RAG structure and framework

Integration with peacecorps.gov website (Django 5/Wagtail 8 CMS); solution must be solution-agnostic and work with current and future peacecorps.gov architecture; Peace Corps will whitelist script-src for chatbot widget via Content Security Policy (CSP)
Multilingual functionality required: English and Spanish at minimum at initial deployment; additional languages evaluated as best value; single English-language RAG dataset must support translation

Live agent escalation and queue management required; contractor provides all queue management logic and escalation routing; Peace Corps has existing team of 10 contractors for Tier 1 support, available 15 hours/day; no existing live agent platform exists
Continuous learning mechanisms required with human-in-the-loop updates; Peace Corps staff must be trained to manage ongoing administration without contractor dependence
Section 508 compliance required (VPAT acceptable as demonstration of compliance)
FIPS 140-2 compliant encryption of data in transit and at rest
99.9% uptime SLA during business hours
HIPAA-compliant handling and redirection of PHI/PII interactions; BAA NOT required
Analytics and reporting: KPI dashboard, usage reports, user satisfaction, response accuracy, adoption rates, containment rate, escalation rate
Data export at contract completion in CSV format; all collected data is Government property
All contractor personnel require background investigations (minimum Tier 1 for network access; Tier 2 for PII/CUI access); all employees must sign NDAs
Exit strategy and data portability: machine-readable export in non-proprietary format (JSON, CSV) upon contract termination
Contractor responsible for Tier 3 post-deployment support; Peace Corps provides all other support tiers
Offeror must propose complete solution including hosting infrastructure architecture
CRM integration NOT required at initial implementation; may be required depending on proposed solution
Section 508 compliance required; VPAT acceptable
No use of Kaspersky Lab or banned telecom/surveillance equipment per FAR 52.204-25 and PL 115-91
CCPA compliance requirement has been REMOVED from PWS per Q&A response to Question 69
AI Risk Management: contractor must describe their process; no specific framework mandated but NIST AI RMF is referenced
Contractor must develop and propose implementation timeline; go-live date determined post-award based on final project plan; ATO must be issued before solution goes into production
requirements_summary.clearance_requirements: No security clearance specified; however, all contractor/subcontractor personnel must have favorable background investigation adjudication: Tier 1 or higher before accessing Peace Corps network/IT systems; Tier 2 or higher before accessing PII/CUI; Tier 2 or higher for privileged access
requirements_summary.key_personnel: Not specified by name or title in provided documents; Q&A confirms vendors propose their own staffing approach and level of effort; at minimum, Peace Corps OCIO Product Managers and IT Specialists must be able to manage solution post-deployment; key personnel past performance may be submitted for emerging small businesses
deliverables: Draft QASP (submitted with quote; serves as starting point for post-award QASP)
Final QASP (within 30 days of start of performance)
Post-Award Kickoff Meeting (within 10 business days of start of performance)
Project Plan (within 15 days after start of performance; maintained throughout)
Data Protection Plan (within 15 days of start of period of performance)
Requirements Specification Document (within 30 days after kickoff meeting; must be approved before design phase begins)
Design and Architecture Documentation (within 45 days following approval of Requirements Specification Document)
Integration Plan and Report (Integration Plan prior to integration; Report within 15 days of successful integration)
Implementation Plan (45 days before scheduled implementation date)
Test Plan (30 days prior to scheduled start of testing)
Usability Testing Report (within 15 days after final prototype delivery)
User Acceptance Testing (UAT) and Test Results Report (UAT completed within 30 days of final design document delivery; results within 10 days after UAT completion)
Training Materials and Knowledge Transfer (10 days before go-live; includes user manuals, training guides, video tutorials)
Final Chatbot Solution (within 60 days of successful completion of final design documentation, testing, cybersecurity assessment, and issuance of Peace Corps ATO)
KPI Dashboard (first report/dashboard within 15 days of go-live; regular updates per agency specification)
Monthly Status Reports (by 10th of each month with invoice, until go-live)
Meeting Minutes (within 5 business days of conclusion of meetings)
Final Report (within 30 calendar days of go-live/release into production)
Limitations on Subcontracting Report - Attachment 2 (submitted to Contracting Officer 60 days prior to end of base period and all option periods)
Monthly vulnerability scans and POA&M (per FedRAMP continuous monitoring requirements)
Monthly report on separated staff (beginning 60 days after option period execution)
Privacy Threshold Assessment (PTA)
Transparency and Accountability documentation
Employee NDAs for all contractor/subcontractor personnel

submission_requirements.page_limit_total: 20 pages for Volume I - Technical only; no page limits stated for Volumes II-V

page_limits_by_volume.Volume I - Technical: 20 pages (cover page and table of contents do NOT count toward the 20-page limit)

submission_requirements.file_format: PDF and Word formats are both acceptable, provided all instructions and formatting requirements are followed
submission_requirements.submission_method: Electronic submission via email; Government encourages all five volumes in a single email but separate emails permitted if file size prevents consolidation; offerors responsible for ensuring complete quote is received

volume_structure: Volume I - Technical (20-page limit; cover page and table of contents excluded from page count; cover letter may be included for each volume)

Volume II - Past Performance (no page limit; three references of similar efforts within last three years required; key personnel past performance acceptable for emerging small businesses; subcontractor past performance acceptable but prime performance preferred; commercial references acceptable if comparable complexity)
Volume III - Assumptions, Conditions, or Exceptions (no page limit; submitted under separate cover)

Volume IV - Draft Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan (QASP) (no page limit; follows FAR Overhaul 46.4; contractor's format acceptable; draft submitted with quote serves as starting point for post-award final QASP)

Volume V - Price (no page limit; FFP required for base period and each option period separately; pricing structure should include: (1) implementation including RAG training, (2) training and training materials for administrators and users, (3) year-over-year recurring costs including usage rate, hosting, subscription, Tier 3 support; SaaS licensing costs included under ODCs excluded from FAR 52.219-14 subcontracting calculation; existing contract labor categories and rates may be used as basis for discounts)
section_l.signing_requirements: Standard Form 1449 is for internal documentation and reference only; does NOT need to be included with quote submission; amendment receipt acknowledgment required
other_instructions: Only certified small businesses eligible at time of quote submission; firms not certified at submission will not be considered
FedRAMP High solution must be authorized or actively in-process at time of quote submission
Subcontracting allowed but must follow FAR 52.219-14; prime must perform >50% of labor; SaaS/commercial software license costs excluded from 52.219-14 calculation and classified as ODCs
FAR 52.219-14 compliance period: by end of base term and then by end of each subsequent option period
Proposed implementation timeline should be included in response and will be evaluated
Attachment 2 (Limitations on Subcontracting Report) submitted to CO 60 days prior to end of base period and all option periods
Reporting formats: CSV required for data export; Word or PDF preferred for reports (taken into consideration in Best Value determination)
All work must be performed within the United States; no offshore/nearshore resources permitted

factors.name: Factor A - Technical
factors.weight: Not specified (relative weight not stated in provided documents)
subfactors: Proposed technical approach and solution architecture
FedRAMP High authorization status or in-process documentation
Proposed implementation timeline (go-live timeline evaluated; sooner is better, not at cost of quality)
Multilingual capability beyond English and Spanish evaluated favorably
Solution features beyond minimum requirements (e.g., additional languages, reporting format quality)

factors.name: Factor B - Past Performance
factors.weight: Not specified (relative weight not stated in provided documents)
subfactors: Three references of similar efforts within last three years
Relevant defined as similar in complexity and magnitude to PWS requirements
Key personnel past performance considered for emerging small businesses
Subcontractor past performance acceptable; prime past performance preferred
Commercial references acceptable if comparable AI complexity, scale, and compliance

factors.name: Price
factors.weight: Not specified (relative weight not stated in provided documents)
subfactors: FFP price for each option period separately (5 periods total)
Price reasonableness determination (Government will perform price reasonableness; price realism NOT confirmed)
Reflection of clear understanding of requirements

section_m.notes: Government confirmed Best Value Tradeoff approach (Q&A #39); specific factor weights and adjectival rating scales not provided in available documents; additional languages beyond English and Spanish, reporting format quality (Word/PDF preferred), and implementation timeline will be considered in Best Value determination; Government will perform price reasonableness only (not price realism per Q&A #110); CRM integration approach evaluated case-by-case; Volume III (Assumptions, Conditions, or Exceptions) submitted under separate cover

competitive_intelligence.incumbent: None - this is a new requirement with no incumbent contractor and no current spend
competitive_intelligence.estimated_competition: Open to all certified small businesses only; no SDVOSB, 8(a), HUBZone, or WOSB set-aside specified beyond general small business; single vendor award; FedRAMP High requirement (or in-process) at time of submission significantly narrows eligible pool of SaaS chatbot platforms; requirement for solution-level FedRAMP High (not just infrastructure) further restricts competition; subcontracting permitted allowing system integrators to team with FedRAMP-authorized platform providers
notable_terms: FedRAMP High required for the ENTIRE SOLUTION including chatbot application and platform infrastructure - not merely the hosting environment; this is repeatedly clarified across 20+ Q&A responses and represents the most significant technical gate

DISCREPANCY FLAGGED: PWS Section 3.1.1 requires FedRAMP High but PWS Section 6.1 originally referenced FedRAMP Moderate baseline controls - Government confirmed in Q&A (#25, #64, #87, #90, #147) that FedRAMP HIGH is required and PWS will be corrected; offerors should be aware the provided PWS text may not reflect the correction yet

DISCREPANCY FLAGGED: PWS Section 3.1.13 references CCPA compliance requirement - Government confirmed in Q&A (#69) that CCPA has been REMOVED from the PWS; the provided PWS text still contains the CCPA reference

DISCREPANCY FLAGGED: PWS Section 6.1 text in provided document reads 'Moderate High' in one location - Government confirmed throughout Q&A that FedRAMP High is required; PWS text appears to have been partially corrected but inconsistency remains in the provided document
Section 1.10 reference to ODCs not being anticipated was revised per Q&A #118; SaaS licensing costs ARE included as ODCs and excluded from FAR 52.219-14 subcontracting calculation
HIPAA sensitivity: High probability of users entering unsolicited PHI/PII; chatbot must detect and redirect but BAA is NOT required
CCPA removed from requirements per Amendment 1
No specific budget ceiling or IGCE provided to offerors

FFP pricing for usage-based SaaS creates significant risk for offerors as interaction volume is undefined and expected to 'dramatically increase'; Government will not establish interaction thresholds or NTE ceiling
Peace Corps uses highly customized Dynamics CRM 8.2 (upgrading to 9.1) with Click/Dimensions; CRM integration NOT required at initial implementation
Peace Corps website built on Django 5/Wagtail 8 CMS; CSP whitelist required for chatbot widget
Peace Corps uses AWS and Azure for cloud workloads; Azure costs remain with Agency if proposed solution uses Azure per Q&A #118
All data owned by Government; CSV format required for data export at contract end
Contractor may begin implementation planning and RAG development during ATO review; solution may NOT go into production until ATO is complete
Live agents (10 contractors) available 15 hours/day for Tier 1 escalation; contractor provides Tier 3 support only post-deployment
Work must be 100% CONUS; no offshore/nearshore resources permitted
Each contractor/subcontractor employee must sign NDA
Vulnerability remediation timelines: critical within 7 days, high within 14 days, moderate within 45 days

bd_action_items.pursuit_recommendation: Best positioned firms are certified small businesses that either (a) hold or are actively pursuing FedRAMP High authorization for a conversational AI/chatbot SaaS platform, or (b) are experienced system integrators capable of deploying and configuring a FedRAMP High-authorized third-party chatbot platform (e.g., via teaming) while performing >50% of labor as prime; strong candidates will have demonstrated federal AI/chatbot implementation experience, RAG architecture expertise, and ability to navigate FedRAMP A&A processes; pure resellers without implementation capability are unlikely to win given the scope of professional services required

risks_and_flags: MAJOR RISK - FedRAMP High pricing uncertainty: FFP contract structure combined with usage-based SaaS pricing and undefined/dramatically increasing interaction volumes creates substantial financial risk for the contractor; Government will not establish interaction thresholds or NTE ceiling; offerors must build all variable cost scenarios into FFP

MAJOR RISK - ATO timeline dependency: FedRAMP High A&A process can take 3-6+ months; solution cannot go into production until ATO is complete; this could compress the base year significantly; go-live timeline is a critical path dependency that is TBD
FedRAMP High requirement at time of quote submission for in-process solutions; solution must be in-process to be evaluated; this gates a large portion of the market

DOCUMENT INCONSISTENCY - PWS Section 6.1 references 'Moderate High impact systems' and 'Moderate' in places; Government confirmed FedRAMP HIGH is required and PWS will be corrected; the provided PWS document has NOT been fully corrected yet - offerors should rely on Q&A responses, not PWS Section 6.1 language
DOCUMENT INCONSISTENCY - PWS Section 3.1.13 still contains CCPA compliance requirement; Q&A #69 states CCPA has been removed; the provided PWS text has NOT been updated to reflect this removal

No page limit specified for total proposal (only Volume I is capped at 20 pages); absence of clear RFQ document in provided package means response deadline is unknown - CRITICAL DATE MISSING from provided documents
No specific NAICS code provided in available documents; relevant for small business size standard determination
RAG dataset size (1,000-10,000 pages) is highly variable and affects implementation pricing and effort significantly
Continuous learning requirement must not require autonomous model retraining; government expects vendor to define methodology; scope ambiguity could affect solution design
Live agent escalation system does not exist; contractor must build complete queue management and routing logic from scratch; integration with eventual CRM may be required in future option periods
Multilingual translation accuracy validation responsibility falls on contractor; 'Golden Glossaries' and translated safety protocols must be addressed by respondents
HIPAA handling required despite chatbot being primarily public-facing; design must include detection and redirection logic
Background investigation requirements apply to all personnel including subcontractors; this could affect staffing timelines and costs
SaaS license costs classified as ODCs per Q&A; pricing structure must clearly separate implementation labor from SaaS/licensing fees
CRM integration may be required depending on proposed solution and will be evaluated case-by-case; this creates pricing uncertainty
cross_document_discrepancies: CRITICAL DISCREPANCY: PWS Section 3.1.1 requires 'FedRAMP High Authorization' while PWS Section 6.1 references 'FedRAMP Cloud Computing Security Requirements Baseline and FedRAMP Continuous Monitoring Requirements for Moderate impact systems (as defined in FIPS PUB 199).' Government confirmed in Q&A responses (#25, #64, #87, #90, #125, #147) that FedRAMP HIGH is required and the PWS will be corrected, but the provided PWS document still contains the conflicting Moderate reference in Section 6.1. Offerors must rely on Q&A clarifications, not the PWS as written.

DISCREPANCY: PWS Section 6.1 text in the provided document uses the phrase 'Moderate High' in one passage (appearing to reflect a partial, incomplete edit), while all Q&A responses confirm the requirement is FedRAMP High only. The PWS document provided does not fully reflect the corrections promised in Amendment 1.

DISCREPANCY: PWS Section 3.1.13 still contains a CCPA compliance requirement ('California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA)'). Q&A response to Question 69 explicitly states 'This has been removed from the PWS.' The provided PWS document has NOT been updated to reflect this removal. CCPA compliance is NOT required.

DISCREPANCY: PWS Section 6.1.8 references PII system authorization at 'FIPS PUB 199 Moderate High level' - this language is inconsistent with the confirmed FedRAMP High requirement and appears to be another artifact of the incomplete PWS correction.

DISCREPANCY: Section 1.10 of the RFQ reportedly stated ODCs are not anticipated; Q&A #118 confirms this section was revised to remove the ODC restriction. SaaS licensing IS expected to be included as ODC line items. The provided documents do not include the full RFQ text to verify the original or revised language.

MINOR DISCREPANCY: Q&A #62 references 'Attachment 2' as the Limitations on Subcontracting Report to be submitted 60 days prior to end of base/option periods, but the full RFQ with all attachments is not included in the provided document package, so the complete attachment cannot be verified.

NOTE: The full RFQ document (Sections 1-5 referenced extensively in Q&A) is not included in the provided package; only the PWS (Attachment 1) and the Q&A document are substantively available. Many cross-references in Q&A to 'Section 4,' 'Section 5,' 'Section 1.3,' etc. of the RFQ cannot be independently verified from the provided documents.

sections.title: Volume I - Technical Approach (20-page limit, excluding cover page and TOC)

PWS Section 3.1.4 - RAG architecture design and content ingestion framework
PWS Section 3.1.3 - Website integration approach for peacecorps.gov (Django/Wagtail CMS)
PWS Section 3.1.5 - Continuous learning mechanisms with human-in-the-loop
PWS Section 3.1.6 - Optimization methodology (response quality vs. computational cost)
PWS Section 3.1.9 - Multilingual functionality from English-only dataset (English and Spanish minimum)
PWS Section 3.1.11 - Live agent escalation, queue management, and routing logic
PWS Section 3.1.17/3.1.18/3.1.19 - Analytics, KPI dashboard, and usage reporting capabilities
PWS Section 3.1.20/3.1.21 - AI risk management approach and explainability
PWS Section 3.1.22/3.3.1 - Data security, FIPS 140-2 encryption, data retention and portability
PWS Section 3.1.23 - HIPAA-sensitive interaction handling and redirection
PWS Section 6.1 - FedRAMP security architecture, ATO process, and authorization boundary documentation
PWS Section 6.2 - Section 508 compliance (VPAT)
PWS Section 3.2.2 - Proposed implementation timeline and project plan approach
PWS Section 3.4 - Deployment approach including training plan for Peace Corps staff
Staffing approach and proposed subcontractor structure (demonstrating >50% prime labor)
sections.guidance: Lead with FedRAMP High authorization documentation and provide a differentiated, end-to-end solution architecture narrative that demonstrates deep understanding of the RAG-based chatbot implementation lifecycle, the ATO process dependencies, and a realistic go-live timeline that is as accelerated as quality allows, since timeline is an evaluated Best Value factor.

sections.title: Volume II - Past Performance
requirements_addressed: Three references of similar efforts within last three years
Recent (within 3 years) and relevant (similar complexity and magnitude to PWS) performance
Federal AI/chatbot or conversational AI implementations preferred
Commercial references acceptable if comparable AI complexity, scale, and compliance requirements
Key personnel past performance acceptable for emerging small businesses
Subcontractor past performance acceptable (prime performance preferred)
Q&A #3 - Key personnel past performance considered for emerging small businesses
Q&A #134, #136 - Commercial and SLED references acceptable
sections.guidance: Prioritize federal government past performance examples that demonstrate FedRAMP compliance, conversational AI or chatbot implementation, RAG architecture, and public-facing web integration; where federal references are limited, supplement with commercial AI examples at comparable scale and security complexity, and include key personnel examples to strengthen credibility for emerging firms.

sections.title: Volume III - Assumptions, Conditions, or Exceptions
requirements_addressed: Any assumptions made in developing the technical approach due to information gaps
Any conditions required from the Government to execute the proposed solution
Any exceptions to PWS requirements or RFQ terms
Submitted under separate cover
sections.guidance: Use sparingly and strategically; clearly document only material assumptions that affect pricing or technical approach (e.g., Government-furnished RAG content timelines, ATO process duration assumptions, CRM integration scope) to protect the offeror from scope creep while demonstrating thorough requirements analysis.

sections.title: Volume IV - Draft Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan (QASP)
requirements_addressed: PWS Section 3.2.1 - QASP covering all task areas and deliverables
FAR Overhaul 46.4 compliance
Performance thresholds for all PWS deliverables
Surveillance methodology
Draft submitted with quote serves as starting point for post-award final QASP (due 30 days after award)
Contractor's own format is acceptable
sections.guidance: Develop a substantive, PWS-aligned QASP that maps each deliverable and performance objective to a measurable standard and surveillance method, demonstrating quality management maturity and reducing perceived performance risk for the Government evaluators.

sections.title: Volume V - Price/Cost
requirements_addressed: FFP price for base period (June 1, 2026 - May 31, 2027)
FFP price for each of four option periods (priced separately)

Pricing structure: (1) implementation and RAG training, (2) training and training materials for administrators and users, (3) year-over-year recurring costs (usage, hosting, subscription, Tier 3 support)
SaaS licensing costs included as ODCs (excluded from FAR 52.219-14 subcontracting calculation)
Labor categories and hours for implementation and professional services
Existing contract labor categories and rates may be used as basis for discounts
Price reasonableness (not realism) analysis will be conducted
Pricing must be consistent with proposed technical solution
sections.guidance: Structure pricing to clearly separate SaaS/licensing ODCs from implementation labor costs, provide transparent line-item breakdowns for all five performance periods, and build conservative but competitive usage-cost assumptions into the FFP to demonstrate both price reasonableness and risk management given the undefined interaction volume growth.

win_themes: FedRAMP High-Ready, Day-One Deployable: Differentiate by demonstrating that the proposed solution holds or is actively in the FedRAMP High authorization process with documented evidence at time of submission, reducing Peace Corps' ATO risk and accelerating the path to go-live - the most critical technical gate and timeline driver in this acquisition.

Purpose-Built for Peace Corps' Public Engagement Mission: Thread throughout all volumes a deep understanding of the Peace Corps volunteer recruitment lifecycle (lead through returned volunteer), showing how the proposed RAG architecture, multilingual capability, HIPAA-sensitive escalation logic, and analytics dashboard are specifically tailored to reduce staff workload on repetitive inquiries while improving applicant conversion - the agency's primary success metrics.

Sustainable, Staff-Empowered AI Governance: Emphasize a handoff-oriented delivery model where Peace Corps OCIO staff gain full operational control post-deployment through comprehensive training, intuitive RAG management tools, and human-in-the-loop continuous learning mechanisms - reducing long-term contractor dependency and demonstrating alignment with federal AI governance and responsible AI principles.
